title: Kubernetes Core Concepts for Developers
excerpt: Learn Kubernetes from a developer perspective: pods, clusters, deployments, and why it matters beyond DevOps.

body.value:
Kubernetes (K8s) is an open-source platform designed to automate the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.

Instead of manually managing containers across multiple machines, Kubernetes acts as an orchestration layer that ensures your application is always running in the desired state.

value.title: Key Idea
value.text: Kubernetes is like a conductor of an orchestra — it ensures all containers (musicians) work together correctly.

value.caption: Kubernetes as a container orchestrator
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Dev[Developer] --> K8s[Kubernetes Cluster]
    K8s --> Node1[Worker Node]
    K8s --> Node2[Worker Node]
    Node1 --> Pod1[Pod]
    Node2 --> Pod2[Pod]

body.value:
Many developers assume Kubernetes is only for DevOps teams. However, understanding Kubernetes provides major advantages:

  • Run production-like environments locally
  • Test scalability and resilience
  • Debug real deployment issues
  • Work effectively with DevOps teams

value.title: Real Benefit
value.text: With Kubernetes, your app can scale, heal itself, and deploy without downtime — automatically.

body.value:
A Kubernetes cluster consists of two main components:

  • Master Node – controls the system
  • Worker Nodes – run your applications

The master node receives instructions and schedules workloads across worker nodes.

value.caption: Cluster architecture
value.diagram:
flowchart TB
    User --> API[kubectl / API Server]
    API --> Master[Master Node]
    Master --> Worker1[Worker Node]
    Master --> Worker2[Worker Node]
    Worker1 --> Pod1
    Worker2 --> Pod2

body.value:
A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It acts as a wrapper around one or more containers.

In most cases, a Pod contains a single container, but multiple tightly coupled containers can share the same Pod.

value.title: Important
value.text: Pods are ephemeral — if one dies, Kubernetes replaces it instead of restarting it.

value.caption: Pod structure
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Pod --> Container1
    Pod --> Container2

value.code:
kubectl get pods
kubectl run my-nginx --image=nginx:alpine
kubectl port-forward my-nginx 8080:80
kubectl delete pod my-nginx
value.caption: Essential kubectl commands

body.value: kubectl is the main CLI tool used to interact with Kubernetes. It communicates with the cluster's API server and allows you to manage resources.

• Self-healing – replaces failed containers automatically
  • Scaling – increase or decrease instances easily
  • Zero-downtime deployments – update apps without downtime
  • Service discovery – automatic networking between components
  • Configuration management – using ConfigMaps and Secrets

value.caption: Self-healing example
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Pod1[Running Pod] --> Crash[Crash]
    Crash --> K8s[Kubernetes]
    K8s --> NewPod[New Pod Created]

body.value:
Kubernetes supports two approaches:

  • Imperative – run commands directly using kubectl
  • Declarative – define desired state in YAML files

value.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-nginx
spec:
  containers:
    - name: nginx
      image: nginx:alpine
value.caption: Example Pod YAML definition

value.title: Best Practice
value.text: Always prefer declarative YAML files for production environments.

body.value:
Kubernetes is not just a DevOps tool — it is a core platform for modern application development.

  • Pods are the foundation
  • Clusters manage distributed workloads
  • kubectl is your control interface
  • YAML defines desired state

Understanding these concepts allows you to build scalable, resilient applications.

title: Kubernetes Deployment Strategies for Developers
excerpt: Learn how Kubernetes deployments work, including rolling updates, canary releases, blue-green deployments, and background jobs with real examples and diagrams.

body.value:
A Kubernetes Deployment is the core mechanism used to run and manage your application containers. It ensures that the desired number of Pods are running and automatically replaces failed ones.

Deployments do not directly manage Pods — instead, they use ReplicaSets, which enforce the desired state.

value.caption: Kubernetes Deployment Architecture
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Dev[kubectl apply] --> API[Kubernetes API]
    API --> Controller[Controller Manager]
    Controller --> ReplicaSet
    ReplicaSet --> Pods
    Pods --> Containers

value.text: Kubernetes follows a declarative model — you define the desired state, and the system continuously works to match it.

body.value:
Deployments are defined using YAML files. Here's a minimal example:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-nginx
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: nginx
          image: nginx:latest

This configuration ensures that two Pods running nginx are always available.

You deploy YAML to Kubernetes using kubectl:

kubectl apply -f deployment.yml

This command creates or updates resources declaratively.
Rolling updates gradually replace old Pods with new ones, ensuring zero downtime.

value.caption: Rolling Update Process
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    v1a[Pod v1] -->|replace| v2a[Pod v2]
    v1b[Pod v1] -->|replace| v2b[Pod v2]
    v1c[Pod v1] -->|replace| v2c[Pod v2]

body.value:
Kubernetes ensures that some Pods are always available during the update. You can control behavior using:

  • maxSurge – extra Pods allowed
  • maxUnavailable – Pods that can be offline
Canary deployments allow you to release a new version to a small subset of users while the majority still uses the stable version.

value.caption: Canary Deployment Traffic Split
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Users --> Service
    Service --> Stable[Stable Pods]
    Service --> Canary[Canary Pods]
    Stable -->|80% traffic| Users
    Canary -->|20% traffic| Users

value.text: Canary deployments reduce risk by exposing new versions to only a small percentage of users.

body.value:
Blue-green deployments maintain two full environments:

  • Blue – current production
  • Green – new version

Traffic is switched only after validation.

value.caption: Blue-Green Deployment Switch
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Users --> Service
    Service --> Blue[Blue Deployment]
    Green[Green Deployment]
    Tester --> Green
    Service -.switch.-> Green

body.value:
If something goes wrong, Kubernetes allows instant rollback:

kubectl rollout undo deployment my-app

You can also view history:

kubectl rollout history deployment my-app

Not all workloads are long-running. Kubernetes supports:

  • Jobs – run once and finish
  • CronJobs – run on a schedule

value.caption: Job vs CronJob
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Job -->|runs once| Complete
    CronJob -->|schedule| Job1
    CronJob --> Job2
    CronJob --> Job3

body.value:
Example Cron schedule (every day at midnight):

0 0 * * *

Key tools for monitoring Kubernetes:

  • Prometheus – metrics collection
  • Grafana – dashboards
  • kubectl – debugging CLI

Useful commands:

kubectl get pods
kubectl describe pod my-pod
kubectl logs my-pod
kubectl exec -it my-pod -- sh

value.text: Always implement monitoring before production deployment — debugging without visibility is extremely difficult.

title: Kubernetes for Developers: Volumes, Multi-container Pods, and Real-world Patterns
excerpt: A practical deep dive into Kubernetes storage, Persistent Volumes, multi-container Pod patterns, and service account security—explained with diagrams and real-world examples.

value.title: From Hello World to Real Kubernetes

body.value:
Kubernetes becomes truly powerful when you move beyond stateless containers. In real-world systems, applications need persistent data, integration with external services, and secure access to the cluster.

This guide walks through three essential areas:

  • Persistent storage and volumes
  • Multi-container Pod patterns
  • Application security using Service Accounts

These are the building blocks of production-ready Kubernetes systems.

value.title: Key Idea
value.text: Kubernetes doesn't replace storage systems — it integrates with them.

body.value:
In traditional systems, applications relied on dedicated storage systems (like NetApp or EMC). Kubernetes keeps the same model but abstracts it.

Your application runs in a container, but the data lives outside of it — on a persistent storage system.

value.caption: Kubernetes integrates external storage systems instead of replacing them
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    App[Application Pod] --> PV[PersistentVolume]
    PV --> Storage[(External Storage System)]
    Storage --> Cloud[(Cloud Storage e.g. EBS, Azure Disk)]

body.value:
Containers are ephemeral — if they crash, their data is lost. This is unacceptable for real applications.

Kubernetes solves this by separating:

  • Application lifecycle
  • Data lifecycle

Data is stored in volumes that live independently of containers.

value.caption: Decoupled lifecycle between containers and data
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Container1 --> Volume
    Container2 --> Volume
    Volume --> PersistentData[(Persistent Data)]

body.value:
Kubernetes uses three main objects to manage storage:

  • PersistentVolume (PV) – actual storage
  • PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) – request for storage
  • StorageClass – dynamic provisioning rules

value.caption: How Kubernetes storage objects interact
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Storage[(External Storage)] --> PV[PersistentVolume]
    PV --> PVC[PersistentVolumeClaim]
    PVC --> Pod
    Pod --> Container

value.title: Important
value.text: A Pod never uses a PersistentVolume directly — it always goes through a PVC.

body.value:
Instead of manually creating volumes, Kubernetes can dynamically provision them using StorageClasses.

This allows you to define different types of storage like fast SSD or cheaper HDD.

value.caption: Dynamic provisioning flow
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    PVC --> StorageClass
    StorageClass --> Controller
    Controller --> PV
    PV --> Storage[(Cloud Storage)]

body.value:
A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes and can contain multiple containers.

All containers in a Pod:

  • Run on the same node
  • Share networking (localhost)
  • Can share volumes

value.caption: Containers inside a Pod share environment
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    Pod --> ContainerA
    Pod --> ContainerB
    ContainerA --> SharedVolume
    ContainerB --> SharedVolume

body.value:
Init containers run before the main application container and prepare the environment.

Common use cases:

  • Waiting for services
  • Fetching configuration
  • Initializing data

value.title: Rule
value.text: Init containers must complete successfully before the main container starts.

body.value:
Sidecars run alongside the main container and extend its functionality.

Example: syncing content from Git continuously.

value.caption: Sidecar pattern
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    GitRepo --> Sidecar
    Sidecar --> SharedVolume
    SharedVolume --> App[Main Container]

body.value:
The adapter transforms output from your application into a format required by external systems.

Example: converting metrics for Prometheus.

The ambassador acts as a proxy between your app and external services.

Your app talks to localhost, and the ambassador handles external communication.

value.caption: Ambassador pattern
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    App --> Ambassador
    Ambassador --> ExternalAPI[(External API)]

body.value:
Applications running in Pods interact with Kubernetes using Service Accounts.

Every request goes through the Kubernetes API server:

value.caption: Authentication and authorization flow
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    App->>API Server: Request (with token)
    API Server->>Auth: Authenticate
    Auth->>RBAC: Authorize
    RBAC-->>API Server: अनुमति/Denied
    API Server-->>App: Response

value.title: Best Practice
value.text: Always follow least privilege — give apps only the permissions they need.

body.value:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: service-reader

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: read-services
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["services"]
  verbs: ["get", "list"]

Real-world Kubernetes applications combine all of these concepts:

  • Persistent storage via PVC and StorageClass
  • Multi-container Pods (init + sidecars)
  • Secure API access via Service Accounts

When combined, they create scalable, maintainable, and production-ready systems.

title: Kubernetes for Developers: Moving from Docker Compose to Kubernetes
excerpt: Learn how to migrate from Docker Compose to Kubernetes using Kompose and Skaffold, with practical workflows, mappings, and developer-focused insights.

value.title: Why Move from Docker Compose to Kubernetes?

body.value:
Docker Compose is an excellent tool for running multi-container applications locally. It allows developers to define services, networks, and volumes in a single YAML file and start everything with a single command.

However, as applications grow and move toward production environments, limitations start to appear. This is where Kubernetes becomes essential.

value.title: Key Insight
value.text: Docker Compose is great for local orchestration, but Kubernetes is designed for production-grade scaling, resilience, and automation.

value.caption: Docker Compose vs Kubernetes scope
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Dev[Developer Machine] --> Compose[Docker Compose]
    Compose --> Containers[Containers]

    Dev --> Kubernetes[Kubernetes Cluster]
    Kubernetes --> Nodes[Multiple Nodes]
    Nodes --> Pods[Pods]
    Pods --> Containers2[Containers]

body.value:
Docker Compose defines multi-container applications using a single YAML file. Each service corresponds to a container.

Example responsibilities:

  • Build images
  • Run containers
  • Manage networks
  • Handle logs

version: '3'
services:
  web:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "5000:5000"
  db:
    image: postgres
networks:
  default:
    driver: bridge

value.title: Limitation
value.text: Docker Compose runs on a single machine and lacks built-in scaling, healing, and advanced deployment strategies.

body.value:
Kubernetes is a container orchestration platform designed for scalability and resilience. It introduces several key abstractions:

  • Pod – runs containers
  • Deployment – manages Pods
  • Service – networking layer
  • ConfigMap / Secret – configuration

value.caption: Kubernetes architecture overview
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    User --> kubectl
    kubectl --> Master[Master Node]
    Master --> Worker1[Worker Node]
    Master --> Worker2[Worker Node]
    Worker1 --> Pod1[Pod]
    Worker2 --> Pod2[Pod]
    Pod1 --> Container1
    Pod2 --> Container2

value.title: How Docker Compose Maps to Kubernetes

body.value: The biggest conceptual shift is moving from one YAML file to multiple Kubernetes resources.

value.caption: Docker Compose to Kubernetes mapping
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Compose[Docker Compose YAML]
    Compose --> Service[Service]
    Compose --> Deployment[Deployment]
    Compose --> ConfigMap[ConfigMap]
    Compose --> Storage[Volumes]

    Deployment --> Pods[Pods]
    Pods --> Containers[Containers]

value.title: Important
value.text: One Docker Compose file typically becomes multiple Kubernetes YAML files (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, etc.).

body.value:
Kompose is an open-source tool that converts Docker Compose files into Kubernetes manifests.

It generates:

  • Deployments
  • Services
  • Volumes
kompose convert

value.title: Reality Check
value.text: Kompose gives you a great starting point, but you will almost always need to tweak the generated YAML.

value.caption: Kompose conversion process
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Compose[Docker Compose YAML]
    Compose --> Kompose
    Kompose --> Deployment
    Kompose --> Service
    Kompose --> ConfigMap

body.value:
Skaffold takes things further by enabling continuous development directly on Kubernetes.

It automates:

  • Build
  • Deploy
  • Monitor
  • Sync changes

skaffold init --compose-file docker-compose.yml
skaffold dev

value.caption: Skaffold development loop
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
    CodeChange[Code Change] --> Build[Build Image]
    Build --> Deploy[Deploy to Kubernetes]
    Deploy --> Pod[Running Pod]
    Pod --> Logs[Stream Logs]
    Logs --> CodeChange

value.title: Developer Experience
value.text: Skaffold enables a local dev loop on Kubernetes similar to Docker Compose—but with production-like behavior.

body.value:
Rebuilding full Docker images on every change can be slow. Skaffold solves this using file sync.

Instead of rebuilding the image:

  • Build locally
  • Sync files into container
  • Instant updates

value.title: Performance Tip
value.text: Use file sync for frontend apps (React, Angular) to avoid slow rebuild cycles.

body.value:
• Docker Compose is ideal for local development
  • Kubernetes is designed for scalable production systems
  • Kompose automates YAML conversion
  • Skaffold enables live development on Kubernetes
  • Understanding the mapping between both worlds is critical

title: Kubernetes for Developers: Moving to the Cloud
excerpt: A practical developer-focused guide to running Kubernetes in the cloud with managed services, Terraform, Helm, and clusters on AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.

value.title: Why Kubernetes in the Cloud Matters

body.value:
Running Kubernetes on a managed cloud service can reduce operational overhead, simplify maintenance, and improve stability. Instead of spending time building and patching the Kubernetes control plane yourself, you use the managed Kubernetes offering of a cloud provider and focus on deploying your application.

This course presents Kubernetes in the cloud from a developer perspective. The main idea is not to become a cloud platform engineer overnight, but to understand the core decisions, tools, and workflows needed to move containerized workloads into AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud.

value.title: Main takeaway
value.text: Managed Kubernetes reduces undifferentiated infrastructure work so developers can focus more on applications and less on cluster administration.

value.caption: From application code to managed Kubernetes in the cloud
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Dev[Developer] --> Code[Containerized Application]
    Code --> IaC[Terraform]
    IaC --> Cloud[Cloud Provider]
    Cloud --> MKS[Managed Kubernetes Service]
    MKS --> Cluster[Kubernetes Cluster]
    Cluster --> App[Running Application]

value.title: What Cloud Means in This Context

body.value:
In this course, the cloud means using infrastructure owned and operated by a cloud provider. That includes data centers, networking, storage, power, physical security, hardware replacement, and large portions of operational support. Instead of provisioning and maintaining all of that yourself, you consume it as a service.

For Kubernetes, this changes the problem significantly. You are no longer deciding only how your application should run. You are also deciding which parts of Kubernetes management should remain your responsibility and which parts should be delegated to the provider.

The strongest reasons for using cloud-managed Kubernetes are agility, elasticity, and cost control. You can provision faster, scale more easily, and avoid large upfront infrastructure investments.

value.caption: Why cloud is attractive for Kubernetes workloads
value.diagram:
flowchart TB
    Cloud[Cloud Platform] --> Agility[Agility\nCreate resources quickly]
    Cloud --> Elasticity[Elasticity\nScale up and down]
    Cloud --> Cost[Pay as you go]
    Cloud --> Managed[Managed Services\nLess cluster maintenance]

body.value:
A major theme of the course is the difference between self-managing Kubernetes on cloud virtual machines and using a managed Kubernetes service.

With self-managed Kubernetes, you are still responsible for installing, upgrading, securing, and troubleshooting the cluster itself. With managed Kubernetes, the provider handles much of the Kubernetes infrastructure so you can work mainly at the cluster usage level rather than the cluster implementation level.

This is one of the clearest examples of reducing undifferentiated heavy lifting. Unless your business is providing Kubernetes itself, the cluster is usually a means to an end, not the product.

value.title: Important distinction
value.text: Maintaining your application is not the same as maintaining Kubernetes. A cluster has its own lifecycle, upgrades, security requirements, and failure modes.

value.caption: Responsibility split
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    A[Self-Managed Kubernetes] --> A1[You manage control plane]
    A --> A2[You manage worker nodes]
    A --> A3[You patch and troubleshoot cluster]
    B[Managed Kubernetes] --> B1[Provider manages core cluster infrastructure]
    B --> B2[You manage workloads and app configuration]
    B --> B3[You still design scaling, security, and operations]

value.title: Cloud Native, Lift and Shift, Multi-cloud, Hybrid, and Serverless

body.value:
The course explains several cloud strategies developers should recognize:

  • Cloud native means designing applications to benefit from the cloud, often by using managed services and flexible, service-oriented architectures.
  • Lift and shift means moving an existing setup into the cloud with minimal architectural change.
  • Multi-cloud means using more than one cloud provider.
  • Hybrid means combining cloud resources with private data center infrastructure.
  • Serverless means consuming execution capacity on demand without managing servers directly.

The course leans toward a cloud-native approach by using each provider's managed Kubernetes service instead of treating the cloud as just another place to host virtual machines.

value.title: How You Interact with the Cloud

body.value:
Another central topic is how teams interact with cloud infrastructure. The course compares four broad approaches:

  • Web console
  • Command-line interfaces
  • SDKs
  • Infrastructure as Code

The web console is useful for discovery and quick experiments, but it creates manual toil and is difficult to reproduce consistently. CLIs and SDKs improve automation. Infrastructure as Code goes further by turning the desired infrastructure into version-controlled definitions that can be reviewed, repeated, and applied across environments.

value.title: Why IaC matters
value.text: Infrastructure as Code makes cloud infrastructure repeatable, reviewable, and easier to scale across environments and teams.

value.caption: Ways to interact with cloud infrastructure
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Console[Web Console] --> Manual[Manual clicks]
    CLI[Command Line] --> Scripts[Scripts]
    SDK[SDKs] --> Apps[Programmatic access]
    IaC[Infrastructure as Code] --> VCS[Version control]
    IaC --> Repeatable[Repeatable environments]
    IaC --> Review[Code review and automation]

value.title: Terraform as the Common Provisioning Layer

body.value:
The practical backbone of the course is Terraform. Instead of creating clusters manually in three separate cloud consoles, the course uses Terraform to define infrastructure and then apply similar patterns to AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.

This is important for developers because it introduces a consistent mental model. The provider-specific details differ, but the workflow remains familiar: define infrastructure, initialize providers and modules, preview changes, apply them, and destroy everything when finished.

value.caption: Core Terraform workflow
value.code:
terraform init
terraform validate
terraform plan
terraform apply -auto-approve
terraform destroy

body.value: The course also explains why terraform destroy is so important in cloud experiments. When you create infrastructure manually, it is easy to forget dependent resources such as load balancers, disks, or networking components. Destroying through Terraform helps remove everything tracked in state, which reduces the risk of paying for forgotten resources.

value.title: Terraform licensing note
value.text: The course uses Terraform 1.5.5 and mentions the licensing change that led to the OpenTofu fork. That is useful context for teams standardizing on IaC tooling.

value.title: What You Need to Decide Before Building a Cluster

body.value:
One of the strongest conceptual sections in the course is not about commands, but about design decisions. A managed Kubernetes cluster comes with defaults, but real applications often need more than defaults.

Before building, you should think about:

  • Kubernetes version support on the provider
  • Networking and IP planning
  • Node types and sizes
  • Autoscaling behavior
  • Ingress strategy
  • Secrets management
  • Certificates and DNS
  • Container registry integration
  • Monitoring and logging

The course makes an important point here: creating the cluster is usually the easy part. Ongoing maintenance, upgrades, and operational discipline are the harder part.

value.caption: Key design areas for a cloud Kubernetes cluster
value.diagram:
mindmap
  root((Cluster Design))
    Kubernetes Version
    Networking
    Node Pools
    Autoscaling
    Ingress
    Secrets
    Certificates
    DNS
    Registry
    Monitoring
    Logging
    Maintenance

value.title: The Layered View of a Cloud Kubernetes Deployment

body.value:
The course presents cloud Kubernetes as a stack of layers, which is a very useful model for troubleshooting and design. Problems do not happen only inside pods. They can happen at the cloud account, network, node, managed service, Kubernetes networking, or application layer.

This layered view helps developers avoid oversimplified thinking when something fails. A broken deployment might not be a bug in the application. It might be a networking issue, an IAM issue, missing credentials, DNS, a node capacity problem, or a provider-specific limit.

value.caption: Layered model of cloud Kubernetes
value.diagram:
flowchart TB
    Provider[Cloud Provider]
    Network[Cloud Network / VPC / VNet]
    Nodes[Nodes and OS]
    Managed[Managed Kubernetes Service]
    K8sNet[Kubernetes Networking]
    Pods[Pods and Containers]
    App[Application and Libraries]

    Provider --> Network --> Nodes --> Managed --> K8sNet --> Pods --> App

value.title: Proof of Concept vs Production Thinking

body.value:
The course repeatedly stresses that the right cluster design depends on the level of service you are building. A proof of concept, sandbox, and production-grade platform should not look the same.

If you are experimenting, a simple default cluster may be enough. If you are serving a critical production system, you may need stronger availability guarantees, more environments, more automation, tighter security, and more deliberate upgrade processes.

The course also discusses environment layout choices such as:

  • Separate clusters in separate accounts
  • Multiple clusters in one account
  • Single cluster with multiple namespaces

There is no universal answer. The point is to recognize the trade-offs in isolation, complexity, operational cost, and repeatability.

value.title: What the Course Builds on Each Cloud Provider

body.value:
The practical part of the course is intentionally consistent across all three providers. That consistency makes it easier to compare platforms and focus on the real differences.

For AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud, the workflow is:

  1. Create a managed Kubernetes cluster with Terraform
  2. Verify the cluster using the provider CLI and console
  3. Configure kubectl access
  4. Inspect nodes and system pods
  5. Install and remove a Helm chart manually
  6. Install the same Helm chart through Terraform
  7. Destroy all resources

value.caption: The repeated lab workflow used across AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Start[Start] --> Create[Create cluster with Terraform]
    Create --> Verify[Verify with CLI / Console]
    Verify --> Kubeconfig[Configure kubectl]
    Kubeconfig --> Inspect[Inspect nodes and pods]
    Inspect --> HelmManual[Install Helm chart manually]
    HelmManual --> RemoveManual[Remove Helm chart manually]
    RemoveManual --> HelmIaC[Manage Helm through Terraform]
    HelmIaC --> Destroy[Destroy infrastructure]

body.value:
For AWS, the course uses EKS with the AWS CLI, kubectl, Terraform, and Helm. Authentication is configured through the AWS CLI, and Terraform uses a popular community EKS module to provision the cluster and node groups.

The demos show how to initialize Terraform, validate the configuration, inspect the plan, apply the cluster, generate kubeconfig for kubectl, and then extend the cluster by adding another node group with a different instance type.

value.caption: Typical EKS-oriented workflow
value.code:
aws configure
aws eks list-clusters
terraform init
terraform validate
terraform plan
terraform apply -auto-approve
aws eks update-kubeconfig --region us-east-1 --name <cluster-name>
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

value.title: What is notable in the EKS demo
value.text: The AWS example highlights how much underlying infrastructure Terraform provisions for you: networking, security groups, node groups, and instances, not just the Kubernetes cluster object itself.

body.value:
For Azure, the course uses AKS with the Azure CLI, Terraform, kubectl, and Helm. Authentication is handled through az login and a service principal used by Terraform. The cluster is then created with a default node pool and later expanded with an additional node pool.

The course also shows that the default system pods differ between providers. In AKS, you can see provider-specific components such as cloud node management and metrics-related services already running when the cluster is created.

value.caption: Typical AKS-oriented workflow
value.code:
az login --allow-no-subscriptions
az aks list
terraform init
terraform validate
terraform plan
terraform apply -auto-approve
az aks get-credentials --resource-group <resource-group> --name <cluster-name>
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

body.value:
For Google Cloud, the course uses GKE with gcloud, Terraform, kubectl, and Helm. The setup includes initializing the CLI, authenticating application-default credentials, enabling required APIs, and configuring kubectl access with GKE-specific authentication support.

An interesting practical detail is that the GKE configuration in the course relies more heavily on defaults, including defaulting to the latest available Kubernetes version unless overridden. That demonstrates an important lesson: provider defaults are helpful, but you should understand when default behavior is acceptable and when it should be made explicit.

value.caption: Typical GKE-oriented workflow
value.code:
gcloud init
gcloud auth application-default login
gcloud container clusters list
terraform init
terraform validate
terraform plan
terraform apply -auto-approve
gcloud container clusters get-credentials <cluster-name> --region <region>
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

value.title: GKE-specific lesson
value.text: Provider-specific authentication plugins and API enablement steps matter. Even when Terraform creates the cluster, local tooling still needs to be configured correctly for kubectl and Helm to work.

value.title: Why Helm Appears Twice in the Course

body.value:
The course uses a simple hello-kubernetes chart to demonstrate application deployment. First, the chart is installed manually with Helm. Then it is removed. After that, the same type of deployment is managed through Terraform using the Helm provider.

This is an important operational lesson. Tools like Helm and kubectl can change the cluster immediately, but if those changes are not represented in Terraform, Terraform state will not reflect reality. That can create drift and lead to confusing plan or apply behavior later.

value.caption: Manual Helm vs Helm managed through Terraform
value.diagram:
flowchart TB
    Manual[helm install directly] --> Drift[Potential drift from Terraform state]
    TerraformHelm[terraform apply with helm_release] --> Managed[Single declarative workflow]
    Managed --> Review[Plan and review changes]
    Managed --> Cleanup[Destroy tracked resources cleanly]

value.caption: Manual Helm flow used in the demos
value.code:
helm repo add hello-kubernetes https://paulbouwer.github.io/hello-kubernetes/
helm install hello-kubernetes hello-kubernetes/hello-kubernetes
helm list
kubectl get deployments
kubectl get services
helm uninstall hello-kubernetes

value.caption: Terraform-managed Helm release

body.value:
The course gives a practical warning that is easy to underestimate: pay attention to the cloud bill. Managed Kubernetes clusters cost money, but the larger cost often comes from related resources such as worker nodes, storage, networking, and load balancers.

Autoscaling can help reduce waste, but it is not a substitute for good planning. Node types, storage sizes, transfer costs, and cluster count all affect the final bill. This is why the course repeatedly emphasizes cleaning up with Terraform when you are done experimenting.

value.title: Cost discipline rule
value.text: In cloud experiments, creation is easy but forgetting to destroy resources is expensive.

value.title: Operational Habits the Course Tries to Teach

body.value:
Although the course is introductory, it teaches several good habits:

  • Use the provider CLI to verify real state, not assumptions.
  • Use kubectl to inspect nodes, pods, deployments, and services.
  • Review terraform plan before apply.
  • Prefer automation over repetitive console actions.
  • Find the problem first, then fix the problem.
  • Expect account limits, quota issues, and provider-specific constraints.

These habits are what turn cloud Kubernetes from a collection of commands into a manageable operational workflow.

value.caption: Simple troubleshooting loop
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Observe[Observe actual behavior] --> Inspect[Inspect CLI, kubectl, logs, console]
    Inspect --> Identify[Identify the real problem]
    Identify --> Fix[Apply targeted fix]
    Fix --> Verify[Verify result]

value.title: How to Evaluate AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud

body.value:
The course does not try to crown a universal winner. Instead, it encourages evaluating providers through the lens of your application and organization.

Important comparison areas include:

  • Default cluster behavior
  • Kubernetes version availability
  • Node types and pricing
  • Storage performance and cost
  • Autoscaling options
  • Integrated registries and CI/CD services
  • Operational familiarity within your team

One provider may be a better fit because of pricing, another because of ecosystem integration, and another because your organization already has strong internal expertise there.

value.title: Practical evaluation rule
value.text: Pick the provider that best fits your operational model, team skills, and workload requirements—not the one with the most features on paper.

value.title: What Developers Should Remember from This Course

body.value:
This course is best understood as a practical bridge between knowing Kubernetes and actually running it in the cloud.

  • Managed Kubernetes shifts important infrastructure responsibilities to the cloud provider.
  • Cloud adds speed, elasticity, and operational convenience, but also cost and platform complexity.
  • Terraform gives you a repeatable, provider-agnostic way to provision clusters.
  • Helm is useful for packaging applications, but using it outside Terraform can introduce drift.
  • Each provider has different defaults, tooling, and operational details.
  • Creating a cluster is only the start—maintenance, security, upgrades, and cost management matter just as much.

If you already know basic Kubernetes concepts, this course gives you a strong foundation for exploring EKS, AKS, and GKE in a practical, tool-driven way.
